topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt like sandpaper under his fingertips. Elias traced the elegant script—his own life, meticulously cataloged, yet entirely foreign. He reread the section detailing his 'defining triumphs,' a list of accomplishments he vaguely recalled, but the prose was so polished, so utterly devoid of the messy, frustrating reality of his actual existence. A slow, cold knot tightened in his chest, radiating outward until his jaw ached. He stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the polished floor. "This is a fabrication," he whispered, the sound thin and brittle in the quiet study. He began pacing the length of the room, his movements sharp, agitated. Every corner seemed to hold a secret, every shadow seemed to mock the carefully constructed narrative they had imposed upon him. He wanted to tear the document in half, to watch the ink bleed into meaningless fragments.

[story 2]

Sarah stared at the printed biography, her breath catching in shallow, rapid gasps. The carefully curated achievements listed—the lauded breakthroughs, the celebrated philanthropic endeavors—felt like stones thrown against her ribs. She ran a hand over her face, feeling the heat rise beneath her skin, a flush of intense displeasure. "They didn't just write *about* me," she muttered, her voice low, laced with a dangerous tremor. She snatched the document, crumpling the edges until the paper warped into a tight, angry ball in her fist. Her knuckles were white. She walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, feeling a profound sense of being utterly undermined, a hollow ache settling deep in her core. She didn't cry; instead, she just stood there, rigid, absorbing the sheer injustice of the deception.

[story 3]

When the curator slid the volume across the mahogany desk, Marcus felt a sudden, sickening drop in his stomach. The narrative unfolding before him was a performance, a flawless masquerade where he was merely the scenery. He picked up the pen, his fingers trembling slightly before he brought it down, not to sign, but to savagely cross out a passage describing his 'visionary approach.' A harsh, humorless sound escaped his throat. He pushed the book away with such force that it skidded across the desk, the sound echoing unnervingly loud. He leaned back, his posture stiff, every muscle coiled tight. His eyes, usually warm, were narrowed to slits, focusing on nothing in particular, consumed by a simmering, deep-seated resentment that felt heavy and suffocating, like being submerged in thick, stagnant water.
